beats.prompt: Quick recall from combinatorics. Match each pigeonhole situation to its result.

pairs.left: N items, H holes — guaranteed busiest hole holds at least
N > H items in H holes — what is always forced?

feedback.correct: Pigeonhole: N > H forces a shared box; the busiest box holds at least ⌈N/H⌉. We will use this as the proof that no search scheme can beat ⌈log₂N⌉ questions.
hints: More items than boxes forces two items to share one.
⌈N/H⌉ is the guaranteed minimum load on the fullest box.
Match: N items / H holes → ⌈N/H⌉; N > H → a collision is forced.

beats.prompt: I am thinking of a number from 1 to 1000. You may only ask yes/no questions: 'Is it bigger than 500?' Decisions are answered honestly. How many questions guarantee you find my number — no matter which number I pick?

500.note: Let's test it — you don't check one number at a time; a good question halves the whole range. You can do much better than 500.

100.note: Let's test it — even 100 is far too many. Halving 1000 reaches 1 in only 10 steps.

10.note: Right — each yes/no halves 1000: 500, 250, 125, …, 1 in exactly 10 steps.

beats.prompt: One idea before we count: what does a single yes/no question actually do?

interaction.title: One bit halves the field
interaction.body: A well-chosen yes/no question splits the candidates into two equal halves and rules out roughly half of them — not just one. That is why the count of possibilities collapses geometrically: 1000 → 500 → 250 → … → 1.

feedback.correct: Each yes/no is one bit of information that halves the search space. Keep this in mind as we count the halvings.
hints: A question that halves rules out ~500 items at once, not just one.
The range shrinks by a factor of 2 with every answer.
Read the card, then tap Continue.

beats.prompt: Warm-up. I am thinking of a number from 1 to 100. Using only yes/no questions and halving each time, what is the minimum number of questions that guarantees you find it?

feedback.correct: Exactly — 2⁷ = 128 ≥ 100, so 7 halvings suffice and 6 do not (2⁶ = 64 < 100).
hints: Count doublings until you pass 100: 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 — that is 7.
2⁶ = 64 < 100, so 6 questions are not enough; 2⁷ = 128 ≥ 100, so 7 are.
The answer is 7.

beats.prompt: Watch the halving game live. Tap Yes or No to each 'Is it bigger than the midpoint?' question and see the candidate range shrink to a single number in at most 10 steps.

feedback.correct: Each yes/no fixes one bit and halves the range. After 10 answers the range collapses to a single value — ⌈log₂1000⌉ = 10.
hints: Halve the range, do not pick one number at a time.
It takes exactly 10 questions.

beats.interviewNote: This ⌈log₂N⌉ floor is the 'information lower bound' interviewers probe with '20 questions' and 'find the fake coin' puzzles — no clever scheme beats counting answer-patterns. Proof: k yes/no answers produce at most 2ᵏ distinct answer strings. If 2ᵏ < N, two numbers share a string and you cannot distinguish them — pigeonhole forces a collision. So 2ᵏ ≥ N is necessary, which gives k ≥ ⌈log₂N⌉.
beats.prompt: Toggle bits and watch the count of named outcomes. This is Lesson 1's 2ⁿ run in reverse: there, n bits named 2ⁿ numbers; here, to tell apart N possibilities you must spend at least ⌈log₂N⌉ bits. The pigeonhole argument shows no scheme can do better.

feedback.correct: k bits produce 2ᵏ distinct answer-patterns. Pigeonhole: if 2ᵏ < N, two numbers share a pattern and no strategy separates them. So 2ᵏ ≥ N is forced — k ≥ ⌈log₂N⌉.
hints: Count the distinct answer strings that k yes/no questions can produce: exactly 2ᵏ.
If 2ᵏ < N, the pigeonhole principle says two numbers share a string — impossible to distinguish.
Therefore k ≥ ⌈log₂N⌉ is a hard lower bound.

beats.prompt: Scale it up. I am thinking of a number from 1 to 1,000,000. Using yes/no questions and halving each time, what is the minimum number of questions that guarantees you find it?

feedback.correct: Yes — 2²⁰ = 1,048,576 > 1,000,000, so 20 questions suffice. The count scales by doublings, not by size.
hints: It is the exponent: how many doublings does it take to exceed a million?
The answer is 20.

beats.prompt: Apply the same method to a fresh size. I am thinking of a number from 1 to 500. What is the minimum number of yes/no questions that guarantees you find it?

feedback.correct: 2⁸ = 256 < 500 ≤ 512 = 2⁹, so 9 halvings are needed. The answer is the exponent 9, not 8.
hints: Is 256 enough? No — 2⁸ = 256 < 500, so 8 questions can only cover 256 possibilities.
You need 2⁹ = 512 ≥ 500. The exponent is 9.
The answer is 9.

beats.prompt: Prove you have it. I am thinking of a number from 1 to 1000. What is the minimum number of yes/no questions — using the halving strategy — that guarantees you identify my number?

interaction.scenario: You are playing a guessing game. I pick a secret integer from 1 to 1000 (inclusive). On each turn you ask 'Is it greater than X?' and I answer Yes or No honestly. You want to guarantee you find the number regardless of which number I chose and regardless of how the Yes/No answers fall.

fields.label: Minimum questions guaranteed to find the number

feedback.correct: ⌈log₂1000⌉ = 10: 2¹⁰ = 1024 ≥ 1000, and 2⁹ = 512 < 1000, so 10 halvings are necessary and sufficient.
hints: How many halvings does it take to reduce 1000 to 1? Count: 1000 → 500 → 250 → 125 → 62 → 31 → 15 → 7 → 3 → 1.
2⁹ = 512 < 1000, so 9 questions are not enough. 2¹⁰ = 1024 ≥ 1000, so 10 are.
The answer is 10.

feedback.correct: One bit = one yes/no = one halving. To distinguish N possibilities you need at least ⌈log₂N⌉ bits — and no cleverer scheme can do better, because k bits produce only 2ᵏ distinct answer-patterns and pigeonhole forces 2ᵏ ≥ N. Next: Group Testing — how binary labels let k mice identify which of 2ᵏ bottles is poisoned in a single round.
hints: ⌈log₂N⌉ is the minimum bits needed to distinguish N outcomes.
Pigeonhole proves it is a hard lower bound: 2ᵏ < N is impossible.
Next lesson: Group Testing (Poisoned Wine).
